verses.text: And the king sent, and they gathered unto him all the elders of Judah and of Jerusalem. And the king went up into the house of the LORD, and all the men of Judah and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem with him, and the priests, and the prophets, and all the people, both small and great: and he read in their ears all the words of the book of the covenant which was found in the house of the LORD. And the king stood by a pillar, and made a covenant before the LORD, to walk after the LORD, and to keep his commandments and his testimonies and his statutes with all their heart and all their soul, to perform the words of this covenant that were written in this book. And all the people stood to the covenant. Guidance: The covenant renewed before the whole nation. Josiah does not keep the rediscovered word to himself. He gathers everyone — elders, priests, prophets, 'all the people, both small and great' — into the house of the LORD and 'read in their ears all the words of the book of the covenant.' The whole nation hears God's word together. Then the king 'stood by a pillar [the coronation/assembly station], and made a covenant before the LORD, to walk after the LORD, and to keep his commandments and his testimonies and his statutes with all their heart and all their soul.' He leads the nation in a public recommitment to the covenant, and 'all the people stood to the covenant' — they joined him in the pledge. The chapter is showing us the proper sequence of reformation: it begins with the public reading of God's word and proceeds to covenant commitment. Josiah does not impose reform by decree alone; he reads the word to the people and binds himself and them together to obey it. The chapter is teaching us that genuine reformation is rooted in Scripture publicly proclaimed and personally embraced — the king reads the book, then leads in a covenant to do what the book says. The chapter is teaching us about godly leadership: Josiah goes first ('the king... made a covenant'), modeling the commitment before calling the people to it, and stands in the most visible place to be seen pledging himself to the LORD. The chapter is teaching us that the right response to hearing God's word is not merely emotion but covenant — a binding resolve to walk after the LORD 'with all their heart and all their soul,' to translate the trembling of conviction into the commitment of obedience. The reformation that follows in this chapter flows directly from this moment: the word read, the covenant made, the whole people pledged.

And the king commanded Hilkiah the high priest, and the priests of the second order, and the keepers of the door, to bring forth out of the temple of the LORD all the vessels that were made for Baal, and for the grove, and for all the host of heaven: and he burned them without Jerusalem in the fields of Kidron, and carried the ashes of them unto Bethel. And he put down the idolatrous priests, whom the kings of Judah had ordained to burn incense in the high places in the cities of Judah, and in the places round about Jerusalem; them also that burned incense unto Baal, to the sun, and to the moon, and to the planets, and to all the host of heaven. And he brought out the grove from the house of the LORD, without Jerusalem, unto the brook Kidron, and burned it at the brook Kidron, and stamped it small to powder, and cast the powder thereof upon the graves of the children of the people. And he brake down the houses of the sodomites, that were by the house of the LORD, where the women wove hangings for the grove. Guidance: Cleansing the temple itself. The reformation begins where the corruption was deepest — in the LORD's own house. Josiah orders the priests to 'bring forth out of the temple of the LORD all the vessels that were made for Baal, and for the grove [Asherah], and for all the host of heaven' — the pagan paraphernalia Manasseh had installed in God's house — and burns them outside the city, carrying the ashes to Bethel (the old apostate shrine, deliberately defiling it). He 'put down the idolatrous priests' (the kemarim, pagan priests the kings had ordained for the high places and astral worship). He removes the Asherah-pole from the temple, burns it, grinds it to powder, and scatters the dust on common graves (the ultimate desecration). And he tears down 'the houses of the sodomites [male cult-prostitutes] that were by the house of the LORD' — the temple precincts had even housed ritual prostitution. The chapter is showing us reformation that starts at the center and works outward. The first target is the temple itself, because the worst defilement was nearest to God — pagan altars and an Asherah idol stood in His own house. The chapter is teaching us that true reformation begins with cleansing the place and people closest to God: before purging the nation's high places, Josiah purges the LORD's house and removes the corrupt priests. The chapter is teaching us the thoroughness of genuine repentance — Josiah does not merely remove the idols but burns them, grinds them to powder, scatters the dust on graves, deliberately rendering them not just gone but defiled and irretrievable. Half-measures leave roots; true reform destroys utterly. The chapter is teaching us that the most painful and necessary reforms are often the ones closest to home — cleansing our own 'temple,' our own worship, our own leaders, before we look outward. Manasseh had filled the holy place with abomination; Josiah would not leave a single vessel of it standing.

And he brought all the priests out of the cities of Judah, and defiled the high places where the priests had burned incense, from Geba to Beersheba, and brake down the high places of the gates that were in the entering in of the gate of Joshua the governor of the city, which were on a man's left hand at the gate of the city. Nevertheless the priests of the high places came not up to the altar of the LORD in Jerusalem, but they did eat of the unleavened bread among their brethren. Guidance: The high places defiled, from Geba to Beersheba. Josiah extends the purge throughout the land, 'from Geba to Beersheba' (the northern and southern limits of Judah — i.e., everywhere). He defiles all the high places where unauthorized incense had been burned and tears down the shrines even at the city gates. This accomplishes what every previous 'good' king had failed to do — the recurring refrain 'the high places were not taken away' is finally overturned; Josiah removes them. The narrator adds a note about the high-place priests: they 'came not up to the altar of the LORD in Jerusalem' (they were not permitted to serve at the true altar, since their service had been illegitimate), 'but they did eat of the unleavened bread among their brethren' (they were still provided for among the Levites — treated with a measure of mercy rather than executed, since these were Israelite priests who had served at the high places, distinct from the pagan kemarim). The chapter is showing us the completeness of Josiah's reform — he reached what no reformer before him had touched, removing the high places throughout the entire territory. The chapter is teaching us that true reformation does not stop at the obvious, central idolatries but extends to the tolerated, comfortable, long-standing compromises — the high places that even godly kings like Asa, Jehoshaphat, and Hezekiah's predecessors had left standing. Josiah dealt with the deep-rooted, culturally accepted irregular worship that the nation had simply lived with for centuries. The chapter is also teaching us a note of measured justice and mercy in reform: the genuinely pagan priests were 'put down' (v5), but the Israelite high-place priests, though disqualified from the true altar, were still fed among their brethren. The chapter is teaching us that thorough reform distinguishes between the irredeemably corrupt and the misguided who can be provided for — zeal for the LORD's house need not be indiscriminate cruelty toward people. Josiah's reform was both sweeping and discerning.

And he defiled Topheth, which is in the valley of the children of Hinnom, that no man might make his son or his daughter to pass through the fire to Molech. And he took away the horses that the kings of Judah had given to the sun, at the entering in of the house of the LORD, by the chamber of Nathanmelech the chamberlain, which was in the suburbs, and burned the chariots of the sun with fire. And the altars that were on the top of the upper chamber of Ahaz, which the kings of Judah had made, and the altars which Manasseh had made in the two courts of the house of the LORD, did the king beat down, and brake them down from thence, and cast the dust of them into the brook Kidron. And the high places that were before Jerusalem, which were on the right hand of the mount of corruption, which Solomon the king of Israel had builded for Ashtoreth the abomination of the Zidonians, and for Chemosh the abomination of the Moabites, and for Milcom the abomination of the children of Ammon, did the king defile. And he brake in pieces the images, and cut down the groves, and filled their places with the bones of men. Guidance: Rooting out the deepest abominations. Josiah's purge reaches the most horrific and the most ancient corruptions. He defiles Topheth in the Valley of Hinnom (Ge-Hinnom, from which 'Gehenna' derives — the place became a synonym for hell), so that no one could any longer 'make his son or his daughter to pass through the fire to Molech' — he ends the child sacrifice that Ahaz and Manasseh had practiced. He removes the horses and chariots dedicated to the sun (Assyrian-style solar worship installed at the temple entrance) and burns them. He demolishes the rooftop altars Ahaz had made and the altars Manasseh had set in the temple courts, grinding them to dust and casting it into the Kidron. And remarkably, he defiles the high places 'which Solomon the king of Israel had builded for Ashtoreth... Chemosh... and Milcom' — idolatrous shrines that had stood since SOLOMON's apostasy (1 Kgs 11:7), nearly 350 years, on the 'mount of corruption.' He breaks the images, cuts down the groves, and fills the sites with human bones (permanent ritual defilement). The chapter is showing us reformation reaching back to corruptions three and a half centuries old. Josiah did not spare even the shrines built by the revered Solomon — long age and famous origin gave idolatry no immunity. The chapter is teaching us that thorough reform deals with sins of every vintage: the recent abominations of Manasseh, the imported solar-cult of more recent kings, and the ancient idolatry of Solomon himself. No sin is too old, too entrenched, or too tied to a celebrated name to be left standing. The chapter is teaching us the special horror the LORD attaches to child sacrifice — the defiling of Topheth is given pride of place, so that 'no man might make his son or his daughter to pass through the fire.' The chapter is teaching us that genuine reformation requires the courage to tear down what generations have accepted, even what a great founder built; Josiah measured everything by the rediscovered word, not by tradition or reputation, and demolished whatever the word condemned, however old and however honored its source.

And he took away the horses that the kings of Judah had given to the sun, at the entering in of the house of the LORD, by the chamber of Nathanmelech the chamberlain, which was in the suburbs, and burned the chariots of the sun with fire. And the altars that were on the top of the upper chamber of Ahaz, which the kings of Judah had made, and the altars which Manasseh had made in the two courts of the house of the LORD, did the king beat down, and brake them down from thence, and cast the dust of them into the brook Kidron. And the high places that were before Jerusalem, which were on the right hand of the mount of corruption, which Solomon the king of Israel had builded for Ashtoreth the abomination of the Zidonians, and for Chemosh the abomination of the Moabites, and for Milcom the abomination of the children of Ammon, did the king defile. And he brake in pieces the images, and cut down the groves, and filled their places with the bones of men. Guidance: Rooting out the deepest abominations. Josiah's purge reaches the most horrific and the most ancient corruptions. He defiles Topheth in the Valley of Hinnom (Ge-Hinnom, from which 'Gehenna' derives — the place became a synonym for hell), so that no one could any longer 'make his son or his daughter to pass through the fire to Molech' — he ends the child sacrifice that Ahaz and Manasseh had practiced. He removes the horses and chariots dedicated to the sun (Assyrian-style solar worship installed at the temple entrance) and burns them. He demolishes the rooftop altars Ahaz had made and the altars Manasseh had set in the temple courts, grinding them to dust and casting it into the Kidron. And remarkably, he defiles the high places 'which Solomon the king of Israel had builded for Ashtoreth... Chemosh... and Milcom' — idolatrous shrines that had stood since SOLOMON's apostasy (1 Kgs 11:7), nearly 350 years, on the 'mount of corruption.' He breaks the images, cuts down the groves, and fills the sites with human bones (permanent ritual defilement). The chapter is showing us reformation reaching back to corruptions three and a half centuries old. Josiah did not spare even the shrines built by the revered Solomon — long age and famous origin gave idolatry no immunity. The chapter is teaching us that thorough reform deals with sins of every vintage: the recent abominations of Manasseh, the imported solar-cult of more recent kings, and the ancient idolatry of Solomon himself. No sin is too old, too entrenched, or too tied to a celebrated name to be left standing. The chapter is teaching us the special horror the LORD attaches to child sacrifice — the defiling of Topheth is given pride of place, so that 'no man might make his son or his daughter to pass through the fire.' The chapter is teaching us that genuine reformation requires the courage to tear down what generations have accepted, even what a great founder built; Josiah measured everything by the rediscovered word, not by tradition or reputation, and demolished whatever the word condemned, however old and however honored its source.

Moreover the altar that was at Bethel, and the high place which Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin, had made, both that altar and the high place he brake down, and burned the high place, and stamped it small to powder, and burned the grove. And as Josiah turned himself, he spied the sepulchres that were there in the mount, and sent, and took the bones out of the sepulchres, and burned them upon the altar, and polluted it, according to the word of the LORD which the man of God proclaimed, who proclaimed these words. Then he said, What title is that that I see? And the men of the city told him, It is the sepulchre of the man of God, which came from Judah, and proclaimed these things that thou hast done against the altar of Bethel. And he said, Let him alone; let no man move his bones. So they let his bones alone, with the bones of the prophet that came out of Samaria. Guidance: The Bethel altar destroyed — a prophecy fulfilled across three centuries. Josiah crosses into the old northern territory and destroys the altar at Bethel — the very calf-shrine Jeroboam I had built to found the northern kingdom's false worship (1 Kgs 12:28-33). He breaks it down, burns and pulverizes it, and burns bones from nearby tombs upon it to defile it. And the narrator makes the astonishing connection: this was done 'according to the word of the LORD which the man of God proclaimed.' Over THREE HUNDRED YEARS earlier, an unnamed man of God from Judah had stood before that very altar and prophesied: 'Behold, a child shall be born unto the house of David, Josiah by name; and upon thee shall he offer the priests of the high places that burn incense upon thee, and men's bones shall be burnt upon thee' (1 Kgs 13:2). The prophecy named Josiah by name three centuries before his birth — and now, to the letter, Josiah burns bones on the Bethel altar. Josiah even notices the tomb of that long-dead prophet and orders it preserved ('Let him alone; let no man move his bones'), honoring the man whose ancient word he was at that moment fulfilling. The chapter is showing us one of the most precise long-range prophecies in all of Scripture, fulfilled with documentary exactness across more than three centuries. The chapter is teaching us the absolute certainty and precision of the LORD's word: He named the reformer, the place, and the deed three hundred years in advance, and not one detail failed. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's purposes span centuries — the word spoken to a faithless Jeroboam at the founding of the calf-cult waited through the entire history of the northern kingdom, its fall, and into the reign of a Judean king, and then came to pass exactly. The chapter is teaching us that nothing of God's word falls to the ground; what He declares, however distant, He performs. Josiah, unknowingly walking in the script written before his great-great-grandfathers were born, becomes living proof that the LORD who speaks also fulfills, in His own time, to the letter.

And all the houses also of the high places that were in the cities of Samaria, which the kings of Israel had made to provoke the LORD to anger, Josiah took away, and did to them according to all the acts that he had done in Bethel. And he slew all the priests of the high places that were there upon the altars, and burned men's bones upon them, and returned to Jerusalem. Guidance: The purge extended into Samaria. Josiah's reformation reaches even beyond Judah into the former northern kingdom's territory ('the cities of Samaria') — now an Assyrian province, but apparently with Assyrian power waning, Josiah was able to extend his reform there. He removes all the high-place shrines 'which the kings of Israel had made to provoke the LORD to anger,' doing to them exactly what he had done at Bethel, and 'slew all the priests of the high places that were there upon the altars' (the pagan priests serving those northern shrines, distinct from the Israelite high-place priests of Judah who were spared in v9). Then he returns to Jerusalem. The chapter is showing us the breadth of Josiah's zeal — his reformation did not stop at his own borders but reached into the old apostate north, cleansing the very heartland of Jeroboam's calf-worship. The chapter is teaching us that genuine zeal for the LORD's honor extends as far as opportunity allows — Josiah did not say 'Samaria is not my responsibility'; wherever idolatry provoked the LORD and he had the power to remove it, he removed it. The chapter is also drawing the historical arc to a close: the false worship that the northern kingdom had built and that had finally destroyed it (ch 17) is now, in its physical remains, demolished by a faithful Judean king. The shrines that 'the kings of Israel had made to provoke the LORD to anger' are torn down by the one king who, above all others, sought to turn the LORD's anger away. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD eventually undoes the works of those who provoked Him — the calf-shrines that outlasted the kingdom that built them did not outlast the reformer the LORD raised up. Josiah's thoroughness, reaching even into foreign-held Samaria, shows a heart wholly given to removing everything that dishonored his God.

And the king commanded all the people, saying, Keep the passover unto the LORD your God, as it is written in the book of this covenant. Surely there was not holden such a passover from the days of the judges that judged Israel, nor in all the days of the kings of Israel, nor of the kings of Judah; But in the eighteenth year of king Josiah, wherein this passover was holden to the LORD in Jerusalem. Guidance: The great Passover. Having torn down the false worship, Josiah restores the true: 'Keep the passover unto the LORD your God, as it is written in the book of this covenant.' The Passover — the foundational feast commemorating the exodus, the redemption that made Israel the LORD's people — is celebrated according to the rediscovered law. And the narrator marks its unique greatness: 'Surely there was not holden such a passover from the days of the judges... nor in all the days of the kings of Israel, nor of the kings of Judah.' Not since the era of the judges — centuries — had the Passover been kept like this. (2 Chr 35 describes its extraordinary scale and care.) The chapter is showing us the positive heart of reformation. Josiah's reform was not merely destructive (tearing down idols) but constructive (restoring true worship). The negative work — removing the false — made room for the positive work — keeping the feast of redemption as God commanded. The chapter is teaching us that genuine reformation has both a tearing-down and a building-up: it removes what dishonors God AND restores what honors Him. A reform that only destroys is incomplete; Josiah completed his by leading the nation back to the central celebration of their redemption. The chapter is teaching us the significance of the Passover specifically — it returns the people to their foundational identity as those redeemed by the LORD from Egypt, reminding them whose they are and at what cost they were made His people. The chapter is teaching us that the climax of returning to God's word is returning to the worship and remembrance He commands. The greatest Passover in centuries was the fruit of the rediscovered book — when God's word is recovered and obeyed, it leads His people back to the joyful celebration of His redemption. (For the Christian reader, the Passover points forward to Christ, our Passover, sacrificed for us — the true and final redemption these feasts foreshadowed.)

Moreover the workers with familiar spirits, and the wizards, and the images, and the idols, and all the abominations that were spied in the land of Judah and in Jerusalem, did Josiah put away, that he might perform the words of the law which were written in the book that Hilkiah the priest found in the house of the LORD. And like unto him was there no king before him, that turned to the LORD with all his heart, and with all his soul, and with all his might, according to all the law of Moses; neither after him arose there any like him. Guidance: The unmatched reformer. Josiah completes the purge by removing the mediums, spiritists, household idols (teraphim), and 'all the abominations that were spied in the land' — and the narrator names his explicit motive: 'that he might perform the words of the law which were written in the book that Hilkiah the priest found.' Everything Josiah did was a deliberate obedience to the rediscovered word. Then comes the climactic verdict, one of the highest commendations in all Scripture: 'like unto him was there no king before him, that turned to the LORD with all his heart, and with all his soul, and with all his might, according to all the law of Moses; neither after him arose there any like him.' This is the language of the Shema (Deut 6:5) — to love the LORD with all heart, soul, and might — and Josiah is declared its supreme royal embodiment. (Notably, both Hezekiah, 18:5, and Josiah receive 'none like him' commendations, for different supreme qualities: Hezekiah for unmatched TRUST, Josiah for unmatched whole-souled TURNING to the LORD according to the law. Together they are the twin peaks of Judah's faithful kingship.) The chapter is showing us the portrait of the most thoroughly obedient king Judah ever had — a man whose every reform flowed from the desire to perform the words of God's law, who turned to the LORD with the totality of heart, soul, and might. The chapter is teaching us the heart of true devotion: not partial or convenient obedience, but the whole-souled turning of the entire person to the LORD according to His word. Josiah is held up as the model of what the Shema commands. The chapter is teaching us that the standard of true godliness is measured against God's written law and fulfilled in whole-hearted love — and that such devotion, though rare, is the highest praise a life can receive. Yet even this unmatched faithfulness, as the next verses reveal, could not by itself reverse the judgment the nation had earned — a sobering reminder that one righteous leader, however great, cannot substitute for a people's repentance.

(Notably, both Hezekiah, 18:5, and Josiah receive 'none like him' commendations, for different supreme qualities: Hezekiah for unmatched TRUST, Josiah for unmatched whole-souled TURNING to the LORD according to the law. Together they are the twin peaks of Judah's faithful kingship.) The chapter is showing us the portrait of the most thoroughly obedient king Judah ever had — a man whose every reform flowed from the desire to perform the words of God's law, who turned to the LORD with the totality of heart, soul, and might. The chapter is teaching us the heart of true devotion: not partial or convenient obedience, but the whole-souled turning of the entire person to the LORD according to His word. Josiah is held up as the model of what the Shema commands. The chapter is teaching us that the standard of true godliness is measured against God's written law and fulfilled in whole-hearted love — and that such devotion, though rare, is the highest praise a life can receive. Yet even this unmatched faithfulness, as the next verses reveal, could not by itself reverse the judgment the nation had earned — a sobering reminder that one righteous leader, however great, cannot substitute for a people's repentance.

Notwithstanding the LORD turned not from the fierceness of his great wrath, wherewith his anger was kindled against Judah, because of all the provocations that Manasseh had provoked him withal. And the LORD said, I will remove Judah also out of my sight, as I have removed Israel, and will cast off this city Jerusalem which I have chosen, and the house of which I said, My name shall be there. Guidance: The wrath not turned — the tragedy beneath the reformation. Here is the heartbreaking 'notwithstanding.' For all of Josiah's unmatched faithfulness, 'the LORD turned not from the fierceness of his great wrath... against Judah, because of all the provocations that Manasseh had provoked him withal.' The sentence pronounced in Manasseh's day (21:10-15) and confirmed by Huldah (22:16-17) stands. The LORD declares: 'I will remove Judah also out of my sight, as I have removed Israel, and will cast off this city Jerusalem which I have chosen, and the house of which I said, My name shall be there.' Judah will follow the northern kingdom into exile; even the chosen city and the temple where God put His name will be cast off. The chapter is showing us the most sobering truth of the whole book of Kings: that even the greatest human reformation cannot, by itself, undo the accumulated guilt of a people determined in their sin. Josiah was the most faithful king Judah ever had, yet 'notwithstanding' his reform, the judgment came. The chapter is teaching us several hard and important things. First, that there is a point of no return in national sin — Manasseh's provocations had so filled up the measure of Judah's guilt (especially the innocent blood, 24:4) that the sentence became irrevocable. Second, that the reformation, though it could not avert the exile, was not pointless — it was right, it pleased God, it delayed the judgment beyond Josiah's lifetime (22:20), and it preserved a faithful remnant who would carry true worship into and through the exile. Third, and most profoundly, the chapter is teaching us that external reformation, however thorough, cannot finally save where the heart of a people remains unchanged — Josiah turned to the LORD with all his heart, but the nation's heart did not truly turn with his (Jer 3:10 says Judah returned 'feignedly, not with her whole heart'). The chapter is teaching us that what Judah ultimately needed was not merely a reforming king but a new heart — pointing forward to the new covenant promise (Jer 31:31-34; Ezek 36:26) of a heart transformed by God Himself, the only thing that can finally turn away wrath and secure a people forever. Josiah's reformation, glorious as it was, could not give the nation that new heart; it could only point to the deeper renewal that God alone, in Christ, would one day provide.

Now the rest of the acts of Josiah, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah? In his days Pharaohnechoh king of Egypt went up against the king of Assyria to the river Euphrates: and king Josiah went against him; and he slew him at Megiddo, when he had seen him. And his servants carried him in a chariot dead from Megiddo, and brought him to Jerusalem, and buried him in his own sepulchre. And the people of the land took Jehoahaz the son of Josiah, and anointed him, and made him king in his father's stead. Guidance: The death of Josiah at Megiddo. The reign of Judah's greatest reformer ends in tragedy. Pharaoh Necho of Egypt marches north to aid the failing Assyrian empire against the rising Babylonians at the Euphrates; Josiah, for reasons the text leaves unstated (2 Chr 35:20-22 says Josiah ignored a warning from Necho that came 'from the mouth of God,' and went to battle in disguise), intercepts the Egyptian army at Megiddo and is killed. His servants bring his body home in a chariot and bury him in Jerusalem with great mourning (2 Chr 35:24-25 notes Jeremiah composed laments for him; all Judah mourned). The people make his son Jehoahaz king. The chapter is showing us the bittersweet fulfillment of Huldah's promise (22:20): Josiah died and was 'gathered to his grave in peace' in the sense that mattered most — he was spared seeing the destruction of Jerusalem, dying and being honorably buried in his own land before the exile. Yet his death was sudden and, humanly, a disaster — the loss of the godly king plunged Judah into a swift downward spiral (the four kings who follow him are all evil, and the kingdom falls within 22 years). The chapter is teaching us the sobering truth that even the most faithful life can end in apparent defeat by worldly measures — Josiah, the great reformer, died on a battlefield in what may have been a misjudged venture. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's mercy to His servant (sparing him the coming horror) can come wrapped in what looks like calamity. And the chapter is marking the true turning point: with Josiah's death, the last restraint on Judah's decline is removed; the brief, glorious reformation is over, and the rapid slide to exile begins. The chapter is teaching us how much a single godly leader holds back the darkness — and how quickly the darkness rushes in when he is gone. The greatest king Judah had left is dead; the end is now near.

Jehoahaz was twenty and three years old when he began to reign; and he reigned three months in Jerusalem. And his mother's name was Hamutal, the daughter of Jeremiah of Libnah. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, according to all that his fathers had done. And Pharaohnechoh put him in bands at Riblah in the land of Hamath, that he might not reign in Jerusalem; and put the land to a tribute of an hundred talents of silver, and a talent of gold. And Pharaohnechoh made Eliakim the son of Josiah king in the room of Josiah his father, and turned his name to Jehoiakim, and took Jehoahaz away: and he came to Egypt, and died there. And Jehoiakim gave the silver and the gold to Pharaoh; but he taxed the land to give the money according to the commandment of Pharaoh: he exacted the silver and the gold of the people of the land, of every one according to his taxation, to give it unto Pharaohnechoh. Guidance: Jehoahaz and the beginning of the end — Judah a vassal again. The swift decline begins. Josiah's son Jehoahaz reigns just three months and 'did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, according to all that his fathers had done' — immediately reversing his father's faithfulness. Pharaoh Necho, now the regional power, deposes him, imprisons him at Riblah, and carries him off to Egypt, where he dies (fulfilling Jeremiah's lament, Jer 22:10-12, that the one carried away would never return). Necho imposes a heavy tribute and installs another of Josiah's sons, Eliakim, as a puppet king, changing his name to Jehoiakim (an assertion of Egyptian sovereignty — the overlord renames the vassal). Jehoiakim pays the tribute by taxing the people, exacting silver and gold from the population. The chapter is showing us how completely and quickly Judah's independence and faithfulness collapsed after Josiah. Within months of the great reformer's death, Judah is a tribute-paying vassal, its king a foreign appointee, its people taxed to enrich Egypt. The chapter is teaching us the speed of decline once godly leadership is removed — the reformation was the work of one devoted lifetime; its political and spiritual unraveling took only weeks. The chapter is teaching us that the sons of even the godliest father may abandon his faith entirely: Josiah, who turned to the LORD with all his heart, fathered sons who 'did evil according to all that his fathers had done.' The faith Josiah embodied did not transmit to his children. The chapter is also showing us the squeeze of the great powers: Judah, caught between Egypt and the rising Babylon, becomes a pawn passed between empires, its kings made and unmade by foreign rulers. The chapter is teaching us that a people who would not serve the LORD freely now serve foreign masters by compulsion — having rejected the LORD's gracious kingship, they fall under the heavy hand of Pharaoh and soon Babylon. The exile is no longer a distant prophecy; its first stages have begun.

Jehoiakim was twenty and five years old when he began to reign; and he reigned eleven years in Jerusalem. And his mother's name was Zebudah, the daughter of Pedaiah of Rumah. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, according to all that his fathers had done. Guidance: Jehoiakim — eleven years of evil under the shadow of Babylon. Necho's puppet Jehoiakim reigns eleven years (about 609-598 BC), and the verdict is the now-grimly-familiar 'he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, according to all that his fathers had done.' (The prophet Jeremiah ministered throughout his reign and denounced him bitterly — for building a luxurious palace by forced labor while neglecting justice, Jer 22:13-19, and for cutting up and burning the scroll of Jeremiah's prophecies, Jer 36 — a chilling contrast to his father Josiah, who tore his clothes at the reading of God's word while Jehoiakim shredded and burned it. Jeremiah pronounced that Jehoiakim would be 'buried with the burial of an ass,' Jer 22:19.) The chapter is showing us the full reversal of Josiah's reign in the person of his son. Where Josiah trembled at God's word and led a reformation, Jehoiakim burned God's word and led the nation deeper into evil and injustice. The chapter is teaching us the stark contrast between a heart tender toward Scripture and a heart hardened against it: the father rent his clothes at the book; the son rent the book and threw it in the fire. The chapter is teaching us that the way a person treats God's word reveals the deepest character of the heart — and that a generation can move from reverence to contempt for Scripture in the span between a father and a son. The chapter is also setting the stage for the climactic events of the next chapters: it is during Jehoiakim's reign that Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon, having defeated Egypt at Carchemish (605 BC), becomes the regional overlord and begins the deportations that will culminate in Jerusalem's destruction (ch 24-25). The shadow of Babylon, foretold to Hezekiah long before (20:17-18), now falls across Judah in earnest. The reformation is a memory; the exile is at the door.

The chapter is teaching us the stark contrast between a heart tender toward Scripture and a heart hardened against it: the father rent his clothes at the book; the son rent the book and threw it in the fire. The chapter is teaching us that the way a person treats God's word reveals the deepest character of the heart — and that a generation can move from reverence to contempt for Scripture in the span between a father and a son. The chapter is also setting the stage for the climactic events of the next chapters: it is during Jehoiakim's reign that Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon, having defeated Egypt at Carchemish (605 BC), becomes the regional overlord and begins the deportations that will culminate in Jerusalem's destruction (ch 24-25). The shadow of Babylon, foretold to Hezekiah long before (20:17-18), now falls across Judah in earnest. The reformation is a memory; the exile is at the door.
